title: AML Agents Briefing — Wednesday, 24 June 2026
script:
ALEX: Welcome to AML Agents Briefing. It's Tuesday the twenty-fourth of June 2026. This is your audio overview of today's most material APAC AML and financial-crime developments. Full detail, the obligation register, and horizon scanning are all at amlagents dot streamlit dot app.

JORDAN: Big day, Alex. We've got on-chain compliance from UBS, AI-powered scam detection going live at a UK retail bank, and an enforcement action out of Australia that every digital currency exchange in the region should be reading carefully.

ALEX: Let's start with UBS. The bank and blockchain engineering firm Nethermind have completed two proofs of concept embedding compliance screening, sanctions filtering and transaction monitoring, directly into the settlement layer of the public Ethereum network. No routing through a centralised intermediary. The checks happen at the protocol layer itself.

JORDAN: So rather than screening after the fact, the compliance logic sits inside the transaction execution. That's a meaningful architectural shift. How mature is this, Alex?

ALEX: It's still proof-of-concept stage, so nothing in production yet. But the direction is clear. Regulators across APAC, including the Monetary Authority of Singapore and HKMA, have been pushing virtual asset service providers to demonstrate that travel rule compliance and transaction monitoring are genuinely embedded, not bolted on after settlement. This is one answer to that challenge.

JORDAN: And the read-across for compliance teams is practical. If you're the MLRO or head of financial crime at a VASP or a bank building tokenised asset infrastructure, add this to your next technology governance review. The question to put to your engineering team is whether your current transaction monitoring architecture can handle pre-settlement screening, or whether you're still relying entirely on post-settlement batch review. That gap is exactly what regulators are starting to probe.

ALEX: Moving on. Starling Bank in the UK has deployed an AI-driven detection layer specifically targeting authorised push payment fraud, covering romance scams, investment fraud, and deepfake-assisted phishing. It's described as one of the first explicit retail bank deployments to name these typologies in the product specification.

JORDAN: The deepfake angle is the one I'd flag for APAC teams. We've seen BNM's typologies and AUSTRAC guidance both reference deepfake-facilitated fraud as an escalating threat vector, particularly in investment scam funnels. The UK is regulated under a different framework, but the typology is universal.

ALEX: So what should listeners do with this?

JORDAN: If you're a head of fraud or financial crime at a retail bank or digital bank in the region, pull your current detection coverage for authorised push payment fraud and check whether your models are specifically tuned for romance scam and investment fraud typologies, or whether they're catching only generic velocity anomalies. BNM's Q1 2026 typology bulletin on investment scams and money mules is directly relevant here. It covers rapid post-onboarding velocity spikes and inbound flows from multiple unrelated retail senders. Use that as a calibration reference against your own detection rules.

ALEX: Now to the enforcement action that will get attention. AUSTRAC has announced a civil penalty of forty-seven million Australian dollars against a major AUSTRAC-registered digital currency exchange for systemic failures across transaction monitoring, mule-account detection, and suspicious matter report timeliness. This caps a twelve-month investigation.

JORDAN: Forty-seven million is a significant number. And the combination of failures is telling. It's not one gap, it's three interconnected ones.

ALEX: Exactly. And AUSTRAC has been consistent in this pattern. When they investigate a digital currency exchange, they look at whether transaction monitoring is calibrated, whether mule-account indicators are being actioned, and whether SMR submission timelines are being met. All three failed here.

JORDAN: For any compliance officer at an AUSTRAC-registered entity, the immediate action is a gap check across those three areas. Pull your SMR timeliness data for the past six months and confirm your average submission time from detection to lodgement. Check whether your mule-account indicators, rapid post-onboarding velocity, inbound from multiple unrelated senders, are live in your monitoring rules, not just documented in policy. And if your annual compliance report for CY 2024 hasn't been lodged via AUSTRAC Online, that's overdue and attracting daily civil penalties under the AML/CTF Act.

ALEX: Before we close, what to watch. Indonesia's PPATK annual AML and CFT programme review for CY 2024 is due by the thirtieth of June. That's six days away. The findings go to the Risk Oversight Committee and the Board of Commissioners.

JORDAN: If you're the compliance lead at an Indonesian-regulated entity, confirm today that your independent review findings are documented and ready for board presentation. The HKMA self-assessment return for CY 2024 is also approaching, due by the thirtieth of September, so if your internal audit validation hasn't started, it should.

ALEX: And for Australian-regulated firms, AUSTRAC's Tranche 2 industry guidance for legal practitioners is now published. If you're supporting a law firm or conveyancing practice through phase two readiness, that guidance covers customer-engagement triggers, source-of-wealth diligence, and legal professional privilege carve-outs.

JORDAN: Three things to do this week. First, review your SMR timeliness and mule-account detection coverage against the AUSTRAC enforcement findings. Second, if you're Indonesia-regulated, finalise your CY 2024 programme review before the thirtieth. Third, take the Starling deepfake detection deployment as a prompt to test your own authorised push payment fraud model coverage. Full detail on all of today's stories, the obligation register, and horizon scanning is at amlagents dot streamlit dot app.

ALEX: That's your briefing for the twenty-fourth of June. We'll be back tomorrow. Stay sharp.
